Since 1977, both H1N1 and H3N2 have circulated in humans as part of seasonal influenza.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) In 1980, the classification system used to subtype influenza viruses was introduced.[[84]](#cite_note-heinen-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Airport_Thermographic_Camera.jpg/250px-Airport_Thermographic_Camera.jpg)](/wiki/File:Airport_Thermographic_Camera.jpg)

Thermal imaging camera and screen, photographed in an airport terminal in [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") during the 2009 flu pandemic. Thermal imaging can detect elevated body temperature, one of the signs of swine flu.

At some point, influenza B virus diverged into two strains, named the B/Victoria-like and B/Yamagata-like lineages, both of which have been circulating in humans since 1983.[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21)

In 1996, a highly pathogenic H5N1 subtype of influenza A was detected in geese in [Guangdong](/wiki/Guangdong "Guangdong"), China[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) and a year later emerged in poultry in Hong Kong, gradually spreading worldwide from there. A small H5N1 outbreak in humans in Hong Kong occurred then,[[44]](#cite_note-steinhauer-44) and sporadic human cases have occurred since 1997, carrying a high case fatality rate.[[17]](#cite_note-li-17)[[62]](#cite_note-vemula-62)

The most recent flu pandemic was the [2009 swine flu pandemic](/wiki/2009_swine_flu_pandemic "2009 swine flu pandemic"), which originated in Mexico and resulted in hundreds of thousands of deaths.[[34]](#cite_note-hastings-34) It was caused by a novel H1N1 strain that was a reassortment of human, swine, and avian influenza viruses.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)[[38]](#cite_note-lampejo-38) The 2009 pandemic had the effect of replacing prior H1N1 strains in circulation with the novel strain but not any other influenza viruses. Consequently, H1N1, H3N2, and both influenza B virus lineages have been in circulation in seasonal form since the 2009 pandemic.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8)[[34]](#cite_note-hastings-34)[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35)

In 2011, influenza D virus was discovered in pigs in Oklahoma, USA, and cattle were later identified as the primary reservoir of influenza D virus.[[10]](#cite_note-sederdahl-10)[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21)

In the same year,[[62]](#cite_note-vemula-62) avian H7N9 was detected in China and began to cause human infections in 2013, starting in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") and [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") and remaining mostly in China. Highly pathogenic H7N9 emerged sometime in 2016 and has occasionally infected humans incidentally. Other avian influenza viruses have less commonly infected humans since the 1990s, including H5N1, H5N5, [H5N6](/wiki/H5N6 "H5N6"), [H5N8](/wiki/H6N1 "H6N1"), [H6N1](/wiki/H6N1 "H6N1"), [H7N2](/wiki/Influenza_A_virus_subtype_H7N2 "Influenza A virus subtype H7N2"), [H7N7](/wiki/H7N7 "H7N7"), and [H10N7](/wiki/Influenza_A_virus_subtype_H10N7 "Influenza A virus subtype H10N7"), and have begun to spread throughout much of the world since the 2010s.[[17]](#cite_note-li-17) Future flu pandemics, which may be caused by an influenza virus of avian origin,[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) are viewed as almost inevitable, and increased [globalization](/wiki/Globalization "Globalization") has made it easier for a pandemic virus to spread,[[34]](#cite_note-hastings-34) so there are continual efforts to prepare for future pandemics[[78]](#cite_note-knobler-78) and improve the prevention and treatment of influenza.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8)

### Etymology

The word *influenza* comes from the [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") word *influenza*, from medieval [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") *influentia*, originally meaning 'visitation' or 'influence'. Terms such as *influenza di freddo*, meaning 'influence of the cold', and *influenza di stelle*, meaning 'influence of the stars' are attested from the 14th century. The latter referred to the disease's cause, which at the time was ascribed by some to unfavorable [astrological](/wiki/Astrology "Astrology") conditions. As early as 1504, *influenza* began to mean a 'visitation' or 'outbreak' of any disease affecting many people in a single place at once. During an outbreak of influenza in 1743 that started in Italy and spread throughout Europe, the word reached the English language and was anglicized in pronunciation. Since the mid-1800s, *influenza* has also been used to refer to severe colds.[[85]](#cite_note-espinosa-85)[[86]](#cite_note-etymonline-86)[[87]](#cite_note-oed-87) The shortened form of the word, "flu", is first attested in 1839 as *flue* with the spelling *flu* confirmed in 1893.[[88]](#cite_note-etymflu-88) Other names that have been used for influenza include *epidemic catarrh*, *la grippe* from [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"), *sweating sickness*, and, especially when referring to the 1918 pandemic strain, *Spanish fever*.[[89]](#cite_note-calisher-89)

## In animals

### Birds

Aquatic birds such as ducks, geese, shorebirds, and gulls are the primary reservoir of influenza A viruses (IAVs).[[17]](#cite_note-li-17)[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)

Because of the impact of avian influenza on economically important chicken farms, a classification system was devised in 1981 which divided avian virus strains as either highly pathogenic (and therefore potentially requiring vigorous control measures) or low pathogenic. The test for this is based solely on the effect on chickens – a virus strain is **[highly pathogenic avian influenza](/wiki/Highly_pathogenic_avian_influenza "Highly pathogenic avian influenza")** (HPAI) if 75% or more of chickens die after being deliberately infected with it. The alternative classification is **low pathogenic avian influenza** (LPAI) which produces mild or no symptoms.[[90]](#cite_note-:AA6-90) This classification system has since been modified to take into account the structure of the virus' haemagglutinin protein.[[91]](#cite_note-91) At the genetic level, an AIV can be identified as an HPAI virus if it has a multibasic cleavage site in the HA protein, which contains additional residues in the HA gene.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) Other species of birds, especially water birds, can become infected with HPAI virus without experiencing severe symptoms and can spread the infection over large distances; the exact symptoms depend on the species of bird and the strain of virus.[[90]](#cite_note-:AA6-90) Classification of an avian virus strain as HPAI or LPAI does not predict how serious the disease might be if it infects humans or other mammals.[[90]](#cite_note-:AA6-90)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Symptoms of HPAI infection in chickens include lack of energy and appetite, decreased egg production, soft-shelled or misshapen eggs, swelling of the head, comb, wattles, and hocks, purple discoloration of wattles, combs, and legs, nasal discharge, coughing, sneezing, incoordination, and diarrhea; birds infected with an HPAI virus may also die suddenly without any signs of infection.[[61]](#cite_note-avian-61) Notable HPAI viruses include influenza A (H5N1) and A (H7N9). HPAI viruses have been a major disease burden in the 21st century, resulting in the death of large numbers of birds. In H7N9's case, some circulating strains were originally low pathogenic but became high pathogenic by mutating to acquire the HA multibasic cleavage site. Avian H9N2 is also of concern because although it is low pathogenic, it is a common donor of genes to H5N1 and H7N9 during reassortment.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8)

[Migratory birds](/wiki/Bird_migration "Bird migration") can spread influenza across long distances. An example of this was when an H5N1 strain in 2005 infected birds at [Qinghai Lake](/wiki/Qinghai_Lake "Qinghai Lake"), China, which is a stopover and breeding site for many migratory birds, subsequently spreading the virus to more than 20 countries across Asia, Europe, and the Middle East.[[17]](#cite_note-li-17)[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) AIVs can be transmitted from wild birds to domestic free-range ducks and in turn to poultry through contaminated water, aerosols, and fomites.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) Ducks therefore act as key intermediates between wild and domestic birds.[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) Transmission to poultry typically occurs in backyard farming and live animal markets where multiple species interact with each other. From there, AIVs can spread to poultry farms in the absence of adequate biosecurity. Among poultry, HPAI transmission occurs through aerosols and contaminated feces,[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) cages, feed, and dead animals.[[17]](#cite_note-li-17) Back-transmission of HPAI viruses from poultry to wild birds has occurred and is implicated in mass die-offs and intercontinental spread.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)

AIVs have occasionally infected humans through aerosols, fomites, and contaminated water.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) Direction transmission from wild birds is rare.[[35]](#cite_note-lycett-35) Instead, most transmission involves domestic poultry, mainly chickens, ducks, and geese but also a variety of other birds such as guinea fowl, partridge, pheasants, and quails.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18) The primary risk factor for infection with AIVs is exposure to birds in farms and live poultry markets.[[17]](#cite_note-li-17) Typically, infection with an AIV has an incubation period of 3–5 days but can be up to 9 days. H5N1 and H7N9 cause severe lower respiratory tract illness, whereas other AIVs such as H9N2 cause a more mild upper respiratory tract illness, commonly with conjunctivitis.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) Limited transmission of avian H2, H5-7, H9, and H10 subtypes from one person to another through respiratory droplets, aerosols, and fomites has occurred, but sustained human-to-human transmission of AIVs has not occurred.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8)[[23]](#cite_note-sautto-23)

### Pigs

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/ChineseFluInspectors.JPG/250px-ChineseFluInspectors.JPG)](/wiki/File:ChineseFluInspectors.JPG)

Chinese inspectors checking airline passengers for fever, a common symptom of swine flu

Influenza in pigs is a respiratory disease similar to influenza in humans and is found worldwide. Asymptomatic infections are common. Symptoms typically appear 1–3 days after infection and include fever, lethargy, anorexia, weight loss, labored breathing, coughing, sneezing, and nasal discharge. In sows, pregnancy may be aborted. Complications include secondary infections and potentially fatal [bronchopneumonia](/wiki/Bronchopneumonia "Bronchopneumonia"). Pigs become contagious within a day of infection and typically spread the virus for 7–10 days, which can spread rapidly within a herd. Pigs usually recover within 3–7 days after symptoms appear. Prevention and control measures include inactivated vaccines and culling infected herds. Influenza A virus subtypes H1N1, [H1N2](/wiki/H1N2 "H1N2"), and H3N2 are usually responsible for swine flu.[[93]](#cite_note-swine-93)

Some influenza A viruses can be transmitted via aerosols from pigs to humans and vice versa.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) Pigs, along with bats and quails,[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21) are recognized as a mixing vessel of influenza viruses because they have both α-2,3 and α-2,6 sialic acid receptors in their respiratory tract. Because of that, both avian and mammalian influenza viruses can infect pigs. If co-infection occurs, reassortment is possible.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18) A notable example of this was the reassortment of a swine, avian, and human influenza virus that caused the 2009 flu pandemic.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)[[38]](#cite_note-lampejo-38) Spillover events from humans to pigs appear to be more common than from pigs to humans.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)

### Other animals

Influenza viruses have been found in many other animals, including cattle, horses, dogs, cats, and marine mammals. Nearly all influenza A viruses are apparently descended from ancestral viruses in birds. The exception are bat influenza-like viruses, which have an uncertain origin. These bat viruses have HA and NA subtypes H17, H18, N10, and N11. [H17N10](/w/index.php?title=H17N10&action=edit&redlink=1 "H17N10 (page does not exist)") and [H18N11](/w/index.php?title=H18N11&action=edit&redlink=1 "H18N11 (page does not exist)") are unable to reassort with other influenza A viruses, but they are still able to replicate in other mammals.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8)

Equine influenza A viruses include H7N7 and two lineages[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) of [H3N8](/wiki/H3N8 "H3N8"). H7N7, however, has not been detected in horses since the late 1970s,[[29]](#cite_note-mccauley-29) so it may have become extinct in horses.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18) H3N8 in equines spreads via aerosols and causes respiratory illness.[[8]](#cite_note-krammer-8) Equine H3N8 preferentially binds to α-2,3 sialic acids, so horses are usually considered dead-end hosts, but transmission to dogs and camels has occurred, raising concerns that horses may be mixing vessels for reassortment. In canines, the only influenza A viruses in circulation are equine-derived H3N8 and avian-derived H3N2. Canine H3N8 has not been observed to reassort with other subtypes. H3N2 has a much broader host range and can reassort with H1N1 and H5N1. An isolated case of H6N1, likely from a chicken, was found infecting a dog, so other AIVs may emerge in canines.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)

[A wide range of other mammals](/wiki/List_of_mammals_that_can_get_H5N1 "List of mammals that can get H5N1") have been affected by avian influenza A viruses, generally due to eating birds which had been infected.[[94]](#cite_note-94) There have been instances where transmission of the disease between mammals, including seals and cows, may have occurred.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-:B22-96)[[29]](#cite_note-mccauley-29) Various mutations have been identified that are associated with AIVs adapting to mammals. Since HA proteins vary in which sialic acids they bind to, mutations in the HA receptor binding site can allow AIVs to infect mammals. Other mutations include mutations affecting which sialic acids NA proteins cleave and a mutation in the PB2 polymerase subunit that improves tolerance of lower temperatures in mammalian respiratory tracts and enhances RNP assembly by stabilizing NP and PB2 binding.[[18]](#cite_note-joseph-18)

Influenza B virus is mainly found in humans but has also been detected in pigs, dogs, horses, and seals.[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21) Likewise, influenza C virus primarily infects humans but has been observed in pigs, dogs, cattle, and dromedary camels.[[10]](#cite_note-sederdahl-10)[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21) Influenza D virus causes an influenza-like illness in pigs but its impact in its natural reservoir, cattle, is relatively unknown. It may cause respiratory disease resembling human influenza on its own, or it may be part of a [bovine respiratory disease](/wiki/Bovine_respiratory_disease "Bovine respiratory disease") (BRD) complex with other pathogens during co-infection. BRD is a concern for the cattle industry, so influenza D virus' possible involvement in BRD has led to research on vaccines for cattle that can provide protection against influenza D virus.[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21)[[25]](#cite_note-su-25) Two antigenic lineages are in circulation: D/swine/Oklahoma/1334/2011 (D/OK) and D/bovine/Oklahoma/660/2013 (D/660).[[21]](#cite_note-asha-21)
